mnemonics.title: subnet Mask Magic Number
mnemonics.content: For /25 to /30: 128, 192, 224, 240. Subtract each from 256 to get hosts: 128, 64, 32, 16 respectively. Think 'Every Good Boy Does Fine' for musical notes, but here: 'Every Great Break Does Fail' for subnet bits.

mnemonics.title: RIP Goes Down Usually
mnemonics.content: RIP = Routing Information Protocol. Memory aid: 'RIP Goes Down Usually' - Remember RIP has limited hop count (15) and slow convergence, so networks 'go down' if not configured properly.

mnemonics.title: OSPF Pays Bills On Time
mnemonics.content: OSPF = Open Shortest Path First. Memory aid: 'OSPF Pays Bills On Time' - OSPF uses Dijkstra's algorithm (like a GPS finding shortest path) and has fast convergence like paying bills on time.

mnemonics.title: BGP Is The Border Guy
mnemonics.content: BGP = Border Gateway Protocol. Memory aid: 'BGP Is The Border Guy' - BGP connects different autonomous systems (like border guards between countries), it's a path-vector protocol.

mnemonics.title: Classful Classes
mnemonics.content: Class A: 1-126 (0 and 127 reserved), Class B: 128-191, Class C: 192-223, Class D: 224-239 (multicast), Class E: 240-255 (reserved). Mnemonic: 'A Big Company Doesn't Forgot Ethernet' - A(1-126) B(128-191) C(192-223) D(224-239) E(240-255)

mnemonics.title: Dijkstra's Dinners
mnemonics.content: Dijkstra's algorithm for OSPF: Start from source, pick shortest path, update neighbors. Memory aid: 'Dijkstra's Dinners Are Delicious' - Think of finding the shortest route to the best dinner spot from multiple options.

mnemonics.title: Bellman-Ford Bounces Forward
mnemonics.content: Bellman-Ford algorithm uses distance vectors and can handle negative weights. Memory aid: 'Bellman-Ford Bounces Forward Iteratively' - It repeatedly 'bounces' through all edges to find shortest paths.

mnemonics.title: VLSM Variables
mnemonics.content: VLSM = Variable Length Subnet Masking. Memory aid: 'VLSM Varies Lengths Selectively Most' - Allows different subnet masks for different network sizes, saving IP addresses.

acronyms.usage: Used to represent IP addresses and their network portion (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24). Replaces classful addressing. Used when calculating supernets and subnets.
Allows subnetting of subnets to use address space efficiently. Applied when different subnets need different numbers of hosts.
Distance vector protocol using hop count as metric (max 15 hops). Uses UDP port 520. Suitable for small networks. Convergence is slow (periodic updates every 30 seconds).
Link-state protocol using cost as metric. Uses Dijkstra's algorithm. Fast convergence. Uses IP protocol 89. Suitable for large enterprise networks.
Path-vector protocol used between autonomous systems (ISP to ISP). TCP port 179. Used for internet routing.高度可扩展.
Cisco proprietary distance vector protocol (now deprecated, replaced by EIGRP). Used within an autonomous system.
Advanced distance vector protocol (Cisco). Uses DUAL algorithm. Fast convergence. Combines features of distance vector and link-state.
Used in routers for filtering traffic. Can be standard (1-99) or extended (100-199). Applied to interfaces for security.
Translates private IP addresses to public. Used when multiple devices share one public IP. Types: Static, Dynamic, PAT.
Maps IP address to MAC address. Used when host knows IP but needs MAC for frame encapsulation. Broadcast request.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a subnet mask as a cookie cutter or filter. The 1s in the mask represent the 'network part' (the cookie shape), and the 0s represent the 'host part' (the dough that gets cut away). When you apply the mask (AND operation), you 'cut out' the network address from the IP.

Picture 8-bit columns as money values: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. To convert binary to decimal, just 'collect' the values where there's a 1. For example, 11001000 = 128+64+8 = 200. Think of it like adding coins.

Think of a routing table like Google Maps. Destination is your destination address, Next Hop is the next turn/road to take, and Metric is like 'traffic time' or 'distance' - lower is better. The router looks up where to send packets like you look up directions.

Imagine each router as a person in a group sharing a secret. Each person tells their neighbors their 'distance' to every destination. Everyone updates their table based on neighbors' info. This is like the 'telephone game' - information spreads but can get outdated (count-to-infinity problem).

Think of each router having a complete map of the entire network (like Google Maps has all roads). Each router knows the state of all links (cost/bandwidth). They calculate the shortest path locally using Dijkstra's algorithm. It's like everyone having a full map rather than just directions.

Think of /24 as a 'street' with 256 houses (2^8), /25 as half a street (128 houses), /26 as a quarter (64 houses). The number after / is like asking 'How many houses on this street?' /24 = 256, /25 = 128, /26 = 64, /27 = 32, /28 = 16, /29 = 8, /30 = 4.

Supernetting is the opposite of subnetting - combining multiple smaller networks into one bigger block. Think of it like combining multiple apartment buildings into one 'complex' address. Example: 192.168.0.0/24 + 192.168.1.0/24 = 192.168.0.0/23.

analogies.analogy: Subnetting is like the postal system. The network address is like the city/district code, the subnet portion is like the locality, and the host portion is like the house number. Just as mail is sorted first by city, then locality, then house, routers use IP addresses hierarchically. The subnet mask determines how granular this sorting is.

Routing algorithms are like GPS navigation. Dijkstra's algorithm (OSPF) is like searching for the shortest path considering all roads (like finding the fastest route). Distance vector (RIP) is like asking locals for directions - they tell you their distance to destinations, and you pick the shortest. The 'metric' is like 'travel time' - lower is better.

A router is like a post office or sorting center. It receives packets (letters) and looks at the destination address (like zip code), checks its routing table (like a delivery guide), and forwards the packet to the next hop (like sending to the next sorting center). If no route is found, it returns 'destination unreachable' (like 'address not found' returned to sender).

Autonomous Systems (AS) are like countries. Within a country (AS), internal routing protocols (IGP like OSPF, RIP) handle delivery. Between countries (AS to AS), you need border protocols (EGP like BGP). Just as you'd use different rules for intra-country vs international mail, routers use different protocols inside vs between AS.

The count-to-infinity problem in distance vector routing is like a rumor mill. If A tells B 'I can reach X in 2 hops', and B tells C 'I can reach X in 3 hops', and C tells A 'I can reach X in 4 hops', they keep increasing forever. Each router thinks it found a path through the other, but it's actually a loop. This is why split horizon and poison reverse are used.

The subnet mask is like an address book that tells you what's 'local' vs 'long distance'. If you're calling locally (same network), you just dial the extension. If it's outside (different network), you need to dial area code + number. The subnet mask tells the router which numbers to 'dial locally' vs 'route externally'.

VLSM is like using right-sized boxes for shipping. If you need to ship 100 items, you don't use a huge container (waste) or tiny boxes (multiple trips). You choose the right size. Similarly, a /24 subnet gives 256 addresses, a /25 gives 128 - you choose based on actual need to avoid wasting IP addresses.
